title: Habakkuk 3
subtitle: Though the Fig Tree Does Not Bud: Theophany and Trust

heb.paragraph: The superscription 'on shigionoth' (v. 1) is a musical term of uncertain meaning, also used in the heading of Psalm 7. It likely denotes a passionate, emotionally intense form of prayer or song. Combined with the closing rubric 'for the director of music, on my stringed instruments' (v. 19), it indicates that chapter 3 was intended for liturgical performance — this is a psalm embedded within a prophetic book.

God's approach 'from Teman' and 'from Mount Paran' (v. 3) recalls the Sinai theophany tradition (Deut 33:2; Judg 5:4-5): YHWH comes from the southern wilderness where he first revealed himself to Israel. The direction is theologically significant: God comes from the place of covenant-making, and his approach in judgment carries the same authority as his original self-revelation.

refs.note: Moses' blessing: 'The LORD came from Sinai and dawned over them from Seir; he shone forth from Mount Paran.'
Deborah's song: 'When you, LORD, went out from Seir... the mountains quaked before the LORD, the One of Sinai.'
David's theophanic psalm describing God's descent to rescue: earth trembles, heavens part, divine warrior appears.

notes.note: The prayer opens with reverent awe, not complaint. The prophet who questioned God's justice in chapters 1-2 now responds with worship. The request to 'repeat them in our day' asks God to act with the same power he displayed in the exodus and conquest.

The theophanic language is cosmic in scope: glory covering heavens, splendor like sunrise, rays of hidden power. The image of light emanating from God's hand, 'where his power was hidden,' suggests that even what we see of divine glory is only the surface of an unfathomable reality.

Plague and pestilence as divine attendants depict God as a king with a retinue of destructive power. The trembling of Cushan and Midian recalls the terror that accompanied Israel's advance through the wilderness and into the promised land.

Calvin emphasized the phrase 'in wrath remember mercy' as the prayer's essential petition: Habakkuk does not ask God to withhold judgment but to temper it with mercy. This is the mature prayer of one who has learned from the dialogue of chapters 1-2.
The theophany teaches that God's past acts of salvation are not merely historical memories but living demonstrations of his character, available to be 'repeated' in the present.

The phrase beqerev shanim ('in the midst of years') is temporally ambiguous: it could mean 'in our time,' 'in the passage of years,' or 'between the extremes of history.' The NET notes favor 'in our time,' reading the prayer as a request for imminent divine intervention.

The phrase qarnayim miyyado lo ('rays from his hand — there his power was hidden') uses qarnayim ('horns/rays'), the same term used for the radiance of Moses' face after encountering God (Exod 34:29). The 'hiding' of power suggests that visible divine glory is only a fraction of God's actual might.

Robertson reads the theophany as Habakkuk's response to the call for silence in 2:20. Having been told to be still before God, the prophet now experiences the divine presence — not as silence but as overwhelming revelation. The God who was apparently absent in chapters 1-2 now fills the cosmos with his glory. The theophany answers the theodicy not with explanation but with encounter.

hist.context: Chapter 3 is a theophanic psalm — a prayer describing God's appearance as divine warrior marching through creation. The prophet opens with awe: 'LORD, I have heard of your fame; I stand in awe of your deeds' (v. 2), then asks God to 'repeat them in our day.' The theophany proper begins in v. 3: God comes from the south (Teman, Mount Paran), his glory covers the heavens, praise fills the earth, rays flash from his hand. Plague and pestilence accompany him as attendants (v. 5). When he stands, the earth shakes; when he looks, nations tremble; ancient mountains crumble (v. 6). The imagery draws on the holy war tradition: YHWH as the cosmic warrior who fought Israel's battles at the Reed Sea and in the conquest. Cushan and Midian (v. 7) represent the nations who trembled before Israel during the wilderness and conquest periods. The theophany is simultaneously backward-looking (recalling God's past acts) and forward-looking (calling for their repetition 'in our day').

sections.header: Verses 8-15 — "You Came Out to Deliver Your People"

heb.paragraph: In v. 13, the purpose of YHWH's cosmic march is revealed: 'You came out to deliver (yeshaʿ) your people, to save (yeshaʿ) your anointed one.' The double use of the salvation root establishes that the terrifying theophany has a redemptive purpose. The cosmic warrior fights not for destruction but for deliverance. The 'anointed one' (meshiah) may refer to the Davidic king, to Israel corporately, or — in messianic reading — to the future Messiah.

refs.note: The Reed Sea crossing, the historical event behind the divine warrior's battle with the waters.
The sun standing still during the battle of Gibeon, echoed in v. 11.
The psalmist's theophanic description of the exodus: 'The waters saw you, God, the waters saw you and writhed.'

notes.note: The battle with rivers and sea alludes to the exodus and Jordan crossing, reinterpreted as cosmic combat. God's military imagery (bow uncovered, arrows called for, earth split with rivers) portrays the divine warrior in full battle array.
Sun and moon standing still recalls Joshua 10 and extends the theophany's scope beyond earth to the celestial realm. The entire cosmos responds to YHWH's warrior march.

The revelation that the cosmic battle serves to 'deliver your people' and 'save your anointed one' transforms the terrifying theophany into a message of hope. The anointed one (meshiah) connects to the messianic hope.
The enemy's defeat with his own weapons is a motif of divine irony throughout Scripture: the oppressor is destroyed by the very instruments he intended to use against God's people.

Calvin read 'your anointed one' as referring ultimately to Christ, in whom God's deliverance finds its final expression. Every past act of salvation points forward to the ultimate salvation accomplished in the Messiah.

The phrase lemashiah yish'ekha ('to save your anointed one') uses mashiah, which can refer to the reigning Davidic king, to Israel corporately as God's anointed nation, or to the eschatological Messiah. The ambiguity may be intentional, allowing multiple layers of meaning.

Robertson identifies the theophany as a recapitulation of salvation history: exodus (vv. 8-10), conquest (vv. 11-12), and ongoing deliverance (vv. 13-15). The prophet who doubted God's justice is now overwhelmed by the evidence of God's saving activity throughout history. The theophany does not answer the philosophical question of theodicy but transforms the questioner: Habakkuk moves from complaint to confidence not because his questions are answered but because he encounters the God who has always acted to deliver.

hist.context: The central section of the theophany depicts YHWH as the divine warrior whose combat is cosmic in scope. The rhetorical questions of vv. 8-9 ('Were you angry with the rivers? Was your wrath against the sea?') echo the ancient Near Eastern combat myth in which the storm god battles the sea (cf. Ps 74:12-17; Isa 51:9-10), but demythologize it: YHWH's combat with the waters is not mythological but historical — it refers to the Reed Sea crossing and the Jordan crossing. Verses 10-11 describe the cosmic response to God's warrior march: mountains writhe, torrents sweep by, the deep roars, sun and moon stand still (echoing Joshua 10:12-13). Verse 13 is the interpretive key: all this cosmic violence has a single purpose — to deliver God's people. The destruction of 'the leader of the land of wickedness' (v. 13b) and the piercing of the warrior's head with his own spear (v. 14) depict the defeat of the oppressor through divine reversal.

sections.header: Verses 16-19 — "Yet I Will Rejoice in the LORD"

heb.gloss: yet I / but as for me
heb.paragraph: The adversative conjunction beginning v. 18 marks the most dramatic turn in the book: despite the terrifying theophany (v. 16), despite the agricultural catastrophe (v. 17), despite every reason for despair, 'yet I will rejoice in the LORD.' The first-person declaration transforms the prophet from questioner to worshipper. This is the existential outworking of 2:4: the righteous person lives by faithfulness, and faithfulness looks like joy in God when every visible support has collapsed.

The closing metaphor of deer feet on high places (v. 19) combines agility with elevation: the deer navigates treacherous mountain terrain with effortless grace. Applied to the prophet, it means that God gives the capacity to navigate impossible circumstances with supernatural stability. The 'high places' (bamot) may also carry the overtone of victory: the prophet stands on the heights that God has conquered.

refs.note: The psalmist's parallel: 'He makes my feet like the feet of a deer; he causes me to stand on the heights.'
David's song: 'He makes my feet like the feet of a deer; he causes me to stand on the heights.'
Paul's command to 'rejoice in the Lord always,' which echoes Habakkuk's unconditional joy.
Paul's contentment in all circumstances as the NT expression of Habakkuk's faith.

notes.note: The physical terror demonstrates that encounter with the living God is not a soothing experience but an overwhelming one. The prophet trembles because he has seen divine power in its full, unmediated intensity. Yet even trembling, he waits patiently.

The catalogue of losses is deliberately comprehensive: every tree, every vine, every field, every pen, every stall is empty. There is nothing left — no fallback, no reserve, no safety net. The verse strips away every material source of security to isolate faith itself as the sole remaining foundation.

These verses are the theological climax of the entire book and one of the greatest declarations of faith in the Bible. The 'yet' (wa'ani) carries the weight of the entire dialogue: after all the questions, all the suffering, all the loss, 'yet I will rejoice.' This is not denial but defiance — the refusal to let circumstances dictate the soul's response to God.

Calvin regarded these verses as the supreme example of faith in the OT: joy that does not depend on circumstances but on the unchanging character of God. This is faith at its purest, stripped of every inducement except God himself.

The deer metaphor teaches that God gives not merely endurance but agility: the faithful do not merely survive but navigate with grace. The 'heights' are not places of ease but of exposed danger, traversed with divinely given skill.

The six 'though' clauses (fig tree, vine, olive, fields, flock, herd) create a catalogue of total agricultural failure using a merism: tree crops, vine crops, field crops, and livestock together represent the entire agricultural economy. Every possible source of sustenance is negated.

The closing rubric lamnatseah bineginotay ('for the director of music, on my stringed instruments') confirms the liturgical setting of chapter 3. The verb lamnatseah is identical to the heading of 55 psalms, firmly embedding Habakkuk 3 in Israel's worship tradition.

Robertson considers this passage the resolution of the entire book's theodicy. The prophet who began by asking 'How long?' (1:2) now declares 'Yet I will rejoice' (3:18). The resolution is not intellectual but existential: Habakkuk does not receive an explanation for evil but a transformation of perspective through encounter with God. The deer-feet metaphor is the final image: the God who shook mountains (3:6) now gives the prophet the ability to walk on heights — the sovereign power that terrified is the same power that sustains. The book moves from burden (1:1, massa) to joy (3:18, agil), from protest to praise, from theodicy to doxology.

hist.context: The book's conclusion is one of the most celebrated passages in Scripture. Verse 16 describes the prophet's physical response to the theophany: pounding heart, quivering lips, trembling legs, decay creeping into bones. The encounter with God's power is not comforting but terrifying. Yet the prophet will 'wait patiently for the day of calamity to come on the nation invading us' — the Babylonian conquest is now accepted as God's instrument, and patience replaces protest. Verse 17 is a comprehensive catalogue of agricultural failure: no figs, no grapes, no olives, no food from fields, no sheep, no cattle. Every source of physical sustenance has collapsed. Against this total material devastation, verses 18-19 declare total spiritual joy: 'yet I will rejoice in the LORD, I will be joyful in God my Savior.' This is faith stripped of every material prop, standing on nothing but the character of God. The declaration fulfills the promise of 2:4: this is what living by faithfulness looks like in practice.

rows.label: vv.1-2
rows.text: Superscription and prayer: repeat your deeds

rows.label: vv.8-12
rows.text: Divine warrior battles waters and nations

rows.label: vv.13-15
rows.text: Purpose: to deliver your people, save your anointed

rows.label: vv.17-19
rows.text: Though... yet: faith stripped to its essence

lit.note: The chapter moves from prayer (vv. 1-2) through theophany (vv. 3-15) to the prophet's response (vv. 16-19). The final declaration (vv. 17-19) resolves the entire book: the prophet who questioned God's justice now rejoices in God's character despite total loss. This is 2:4 incarnated.

themes.note: Faith and sovereignty share the maximum score: the chapter demonstrates both God's cosmic power (theophany) and the prophet's unconditional trust (3:17-19). Worship scores high because the chapter is a liturgical psalm of praise.

hebtext.note: The superscription 'on shigionoth' (v. 1) is a musical term of uncertain meaning, also used in the heading of Psalm 7. trans.title: Comparing translations in Though the Fig Tree Does Not Bud: Theophany and Trust

translations.text: Lord, I have heard of your fame; I stand in awe of your deeds, Lord. Repeat them in our day, in our time make them known; in wrath remember mercy.
O LORD, I have heard the report of you, and your work, O LORD, do I fear. In the midst of the years revive it; in the midst of the years make it known; in wrath remember mercy.
God came from Teman, the Holy One from Mount Paran. His glory covered the heavens and his praise filled the earth.
God came from Teman, and the Holy One from Mount Paran. Selah His splendor covered the heavens, and the earth was full of his praise.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Though the Fig Tree Does Not Bud within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

coaching.tip: Habakkuk's prayer (vv. 2-15) is a theophany psalm — God marching from Teman and Paran (Sinai's region), nature convulsing at his approach. This recalls Judges 5 and Deuteronomy 33. The prophet prays by remembering: 'In wrath remember mercy' (v. 2). Past deliverance grounds present hope.

'Though the fig tree should not blossom... yet I will rejoice in the LORD' (vv. 17-18). Total agricultural failure — figs, vines, olives, fields, flocks, herds — yet joy. This is faith's summit: not joy because of blessings but joy in the God who blesses. The circumstances are worst-case; the trust is unconditional.
